title: Human Evals
lesson:
Human evals means having real people judge how good your AI's answers are, instead of relying only on automated tests. A model can ace every benchmark (a standardized test with a fixed answer key) and still feel clunky, cold, or subtly wrong to an actual user. Human evals catch the gap between "scored well on paper" and "people actually like using this."

Why machines can't judge everything

Benchmarks work great when there's one right answer: 2+2 is 4, this code compiles or it doesn't. But most real product questions have no answer key. Is this summary clear? Is this reply warm but not creepy? Did the chatbot dodge the question politely? You can't grade "warmth" with a regex (a text-matching pattern). You need a human nose.

Analogy 1 — the cooking show. A food-safety inspector checks measurable things: temperature, allergens, cleanliness. That's a benchmark — pass or fail, objective. But whether the dish actually tastes good needs a judge to put a fork in their mouth. Human evals are the taste test. The inspector keeps you out of jail; the taste test keeps customers coming back. You want both.

The main flavors of human eval

  • Likert rating: a person scores one answer on a scale, e.g. 1 (terrible) to 5 (excellent). Simple, but people's internal "4" drifts over time and differs between raters.
  • Pairwise comparison: show two answers (A from your old model, B from the new one), ask "which is better?" Humans are far more reliable at comparing than at scoring in a vacuum — just like it's easier to say which of two coffees you prefer than to rate one coffee 7.3/10.
  • Rubric scoring: a checklist of specific traits (factually correct? followed the format? polite? complete?) scored separately, so feedback is concrete and actionable.

Analogy 2 — the blind taste test (Pepsi vs. Coke). Pairwise comparison is exactly the supermarket taste test: two unlabeled cups, "point at the one you like more." Hide which cup is which (blind) and randomize left/right, or people just pick the brand they already love. Same with models — never let a rater know which answer came from the model you're hoping wins.

A real-world setup

Say you tuned a support bot with DPO and want proof it's better. You collect 200 real user questions, generate an answer from the old model and the new one, shuffle them, and hand each pair to several raters. You count win rate. A simple judging schema looks like this:

{
  "prompt": "How do I cancel my subscription?",
  "answer_a": "Go to Settings > Billing > Cancel.",
  "answer_b": "I'm sorry to hear that! You can cancel under Settings.",
  "verdict": "B",
  "reason": "warmer tone, still gives the steps"
}

Because no two raters agree perfectly, you measure inter-rater agreement (how often judges give the same verdict). If agreement is low, your question is vague — fix the instructions before trusting the scores. Always collect at least 3 ratings per item and take the majority; one person having a bad day shouldn't decide your launch.

Crunching the numbers

You don't just eyeball it. A 52% win rate over 60 examples could easily be noise. Run a quick check on whether the difference is real:

wins, n = 118, 200          # B beat A in 118 of 200 pairs
from scipy.stats import binomtest
p = binomtest(wins, n, 0.5).pvalue
print(round(wins / n, 3), p)   # 0.59 win rate, p tells us if it's real
# small p (e.g. < 0.05) = unlikely to be a fluke

A 59% win rate with a small p-value (the chance the result is just luck) means the new model genuinely won. A 51% win rate over 30 items means nothing — get more data.

LLM-as-judge: cheaper, not a replacement

Human raters are slow and expensive, so teams now use a strong model to play judge on the easy cases — this is called LLM-as-judge. It's great for scaling up, but it inherits the judge model's blind spots (it tends to prefer longer, more confident answers even when they're wrong). The standard play: let the model judge thousands of examples cheaply, then have humans spot-check a sample to make sure the robot judge agrees with real people. Pure automation here is a trap.

Benchmarks / auto-metricsHuman evals
Fast, cheap, repeatableSlow, costs money and time
Need a fixed answer keyJudge open-ended, subjective quality
Can be gamed or memorizedCatch tone, safety, "feel"
Great for regression checksBest for launch decisions

💡 Use benchmarks for fast daily checks, and human evals for the high-stakes "should we ship this?" call. They answer different questions.
mentalModel: Benchmarks are the food-safety inspector (objective, pass/fail); human evals are the taste test (does it actually taste good?). For anything subjective — tone, helpfulness, 'feel' — you need a person to take a bite, and you trust comparisons ('A or B?') far more than lonely scores ('rate this 1–5').
mistakes: Not blinding or randomizing. If raters can tell which answer is from your new model, or it's always on the left, they'll be biased toward it. Hide the source and shuffle the order every time.

Trusting one rater. One person's mood, taste, or misreading shouldn't decide anything. Collect at least 3 ratings per item, take the majority, and measure inter-rater agreement to catch vague questions.
Reading too much into tiny win rates. 53% over 40 examples is almost certainly noise. Run a significance check (a small p-value) and gather enough items before declaring a winner.

Vague rubrics. Asking "is this good?" gets mush. Give raters concrete, separate criteria (correct? followed format? polite? complete?) so verdicts are consistent and the feedback tells you what to fix.

Treating LLM-as-judge as a full replacement. A model judge is cheap but shares model biases — it favors long, confident answers. Use it to scale, then have humans spot-check that it agrees with real people.

Only evaluating happy-path prompts. If your test set is all easy questions, you'll miss the rude, tricky, or adversarial inputs where models fail. Deliberately include hard and edge cases pulled from real usage.
exercise:
Pick any task two models can do — say summarizing a news paragraph. Generate an answer from two different models (or the same model at two temperatures) for 20 prompts. Build a tiny spreadsheet with columns prompt, answer_a, answer_b, and verdict. Crucially: shuffle which model is A vs. B per row so you can't cheat. Ask a friend (and yourself, separately) to fill in the verdict blind, then compute the win rate and how often you two agreed.

Stretch goal: Write a short LLM-as-judge prompt that asks a strong model to pick the better answer with a one-line reason, run it over the same 20 pairs, and compare its verdicts to your human ones. How often does the robot judge agree with the humans — and does it lean toward the longer answer?
solution: Hint: To shuffle safely, flip a coin per row to decide whether model 1 goes in column A or B, and record the true mapping in a separate hidden column you only reveal after judging. Win rate is just (B wins) / (total) — but with 20 items, expect wide error bars; a 12-of-20 result (60%) is suggestive, not proof. Agreement between you and your friend is the fraction of rows where both verdicts match; under ~70% means your judging instructions are too vague — tighten them. For the LLM judge, you'll often find it agrees with humans on clear cases but drifts on close calls and quietly prefers the longer, more assertive answer even when it adds nothing. That's the bias you're checking for.
summary: Human evals use real people to judge the subjective quality benchmarks can't measure — tone, helpfulness, safety, overall 'feel.' Prefer pairwise comparisons (A vs. B) over lonely 1–5 scores, always blind and randomize, collect multiple ratings per item, and run a significance check before trusting a win rate. LLM-as-judge scales this cheaply but shares model biases, so keep humans in the loop to spot-check.

keyTerms.def: Having real people judge an AI's outputs for quality, used to measure subjective things (tone, helpfulness, safety) that automated benchmarks can't score.
Showing a rater two answers and asking which is better. More reliable than absolute scoring because humans compare more consistently than they rate alone.
Scoring a single answer on a fixed scale (e.g. 1–5). Simple, but ratings drift over time and vary a lot between people.
A checklist of specific traits (correct? followed format? polite?) scored separately, so evaluations are consistent and the feedback is concrete.
Hiding which model produced each answer (and randomizing order) so raters aren't biased toward the model you hope will win.
How often different judges give the same verdict. Low agreement signals a vague question or task, not a clear winner.
The fraction of comparisons your model won. Only trust it after a significance check confirms it isn't just luck.
The chance a result happened by luck. A small p-value (e.g. below 0.05) means a win rate difference is likely real, not noise.
Using a strong language model to grade or compare answers automatically. Cheap and scalable, but inherits model biases (favors long, confident replies), so humans must spot-check it.
